Why Regular Oil Changes Matter More During Central Florida's Summer Heat — Oil Change in Deltona, FL

July 5, 2026
LP & Sons Auto Care Team
No Comments

If you drive in Deltona, FL, you already know what a Central Florida summer feels like: temperatures that climb into the mid-90s before noon, humidity that makes the heat feel even worse, and pavement that radiates warmth long after the sun goes down. What you may not realize is that your engine is feeling every degree of it — and the oil circulating through that engine is breaking down faster than it would almost anywhere else in the country. Staying current on your oil change service is one of the single most effective things you can do to protect your vehicle during the Florida summer months. At LP & Sons Auto Care, we see firsthand what happens when drivers let oil service slide — and we're here to make sure that doesn't happen to your car.

How Florida's Summer Heat Accelerates Engine Oil Breakdown

Motor oil has one primary job: to keep metal engine components from grinding against each other by maintaining a protective film between moving parts. To do that job, oil has to stay within a certain viscosity range — thick enough to create a barrier, thin enough to flow freely through the engine's lubrication passages.

Heat is oil's enemy. As engine temperature rises, oil thins out and its protective film weakens. In moderate climates, this is manageable — engines are designed to run hot, and quality oil formulations account for normal operating temperatures. But in Central Florida's summer, the story changes. Ambient temperatures regularly push into the 90s, and when you factor in stop-and-go traffic on I-4 or surface streets around Volusia County, engine temperatures climb higher and hold there longer than in most other regions.

Prolonged heat exposure does two things to engine oil. First, it accelerates oxidation — a chemical process that causes oil to thicken, become acidic, and form deposits inside the engine. Second, it depletes the additive packages that manufacturers blend into oil to protect against corrosion, foaming, and wear. Once those additives are gone, even oil that looks clean is providing significantly less protection than it should.

The result for Florida drivers is that oil reaches the end of its useful service life faster than the calendar might suggest. Waiting for a mileage sticker reminder alone — especially if that sticker was set for average conditions — can mean your engine is running on degraded oil during the hottest weeks of the year, when it needs protection the most.

Conventional vs. Full Synthetic Oil: Which Is Right for Florida Drivers?

Not all motor oil is created equal, and for drivers dealing with Florida's summer conditions, the choice between conventional and full synthetic oil is worth understanding before your next service appointment.

Conventional oil is derived directly from crude oil. It works well in moderate climates and in vehicles that don't place heavy demands on their lubrication systems. However, conventional oil oxidizes and breaks down more quickly under sustained heat, which makes it a less ideal choice for year-round Florida driving — particularly during summer.

Full synthetic oil is engineered from the ground up for performance under stress. Its molecular structure is more uniform than conventional oil, which means it resists oxidation better, maintains its viscosity over a wider temperature range, and stays cleaner inside the engine longer. For a vehicle spending its days in Volusia County heat, full synthetic oil simply holds up better between service intervals.

There's also a middle ground: synthetic blend oil, which mixes conventional base stock with synthetic components. It offers better heat resistance than pure conventional oil at a lower price point than full synthetic — a reasonable option depending on your vehicle, driving habits, and manufacturer recommendation.

The right choice for your specific vehicle depends on what your owner's manual recommends, your mileage, your typical driving patterns, and your vehicle's specific requirements. At LP & Sons Auto Care, our technicians walk through those factors with you so you leave with oil that's actually matched to your needs — not just whatever happens to be the most convenient option on the shelf.

Warning Signs Your Car Is Overdue for an Oil Change

Modern vehicles have oil life monitoring systems that do a reasonable job of tracking when service is due. But monitoring systems can be wrong, especially in unusual driving conditions — and Florida summer driving is unusual driving by most standards. It pays to know the signs that your oil needs attention regardless of what the dashboard says.

  • Dark, dirty oil on the dipstick: Fresh oil is amber-colored and slightly translucent. Oil that has been in the engine too long turns dark brown or black and may feel gritty between your fingers. If you pull the dipstick and the oil looks more like used cooking grease than clean amber fluid, it's overdue.
  • Low oil level: Some oil consumption between service intervals is normal, especially in older engines. But if you're regularly running low before your next scheduled change, your engine may have a leak or an internal consumption issue — and running low on oil in summer heat magnifies the risk considerably.
  • Engine noise on startup: Properly lubricated engines start up quietly. A knocking, ticking, or rattling sound when you first turn the key — especially in the morning — can indicate that oil isn't providing adequate protection. This is especially noticeable with degraded oil in hot climates, where thermal breakdown is more severe.
  • Oil pressure warning light: If your oil pressure light comes on, treat it as an urgent warning, not a reminder. Low oil pressure means critical engine components are not being adequately lubricated, and continuing to drive can cause serious damage in minutes.
  • Burning oil smell inside the cabin: A burning smell — particularly one that resembles something hot and acrid — can indicate oil leaking onto hot engine components. This is both an oil condition issue and a potential fire concern that deserves immediate attention.
  • Reduced fuel economy: When oil is degraded and can't lubricate efficiently, the engine works harder. That extra effort shows up as increased fuel consumption. If your mileage has dropped noticeably without an obvious cause, the oil condition is one of the first things to check.

Any of these signs warrant a visit to LP & Sons Auto Care for an inspection. In many cases, an overdue oil change is the entire issue — caught early, it's a simple fix. Caught late, it can mean engine damage that runs into thousands of dollars.

What's Included in a Full-Service Oil Change at LP & Sons

An oil change at LP & Sons Auto Care is not a five-minute drive-through service. It's a full-service appointment designed to give you an accurate picture of your vehicle's condition while we have it in the bay.

Here's what we do during every oil change service:

  • Drain old oil and replace the oil filter: We remove all of the old oil — not just top it off — and install a fresh filter. An old filter can contaminate new oil almost immediately, so filter replacement every time is standard practice.
  • Fill with the correct oil type and viscosity: We match the oil to your manufacturer's specification and factor in your driving conditions. If full synthetic is what your engine needs to hold up through a Florida summer, that's what goes in.
  • Check and top off fluids: We inspect and top off brake fluid, coolant, power steering fluid, and windshield washer fluid while we have the hood up. In summer heat, coolant levels and condition are particularly important, and we check them as part of every visit.
  • Inspect belts, hoses, and filters: We visually inspect the belts, radiator hoses, and air filter for wear or deterioration. Florida's heat accelerates rubber and fabric degradation, so these components deserve attention at every service.
  • Tire pressure check: Heat causes tire pressure to fluctuate. We check all four tires and the spare to make sure you're running at the correct pressure.
  • Multipoint inspection: Our technicians perform a comprehensive multipoint inspection on every vehicle we service — checking brakes, suspension, lights, and other key systems so you leave knowing the full condition of your vehicle, not just the oil.

This thorough approach is what separates a professional auto repair service from a quick-lube transaction. When you drive out of LP & Sons, you know your car has been looked at by people who actually care about what they find.

How Often Should Deltona Drivers Change Their Oil?

The old rule of thumb — every 3,000 miles — was developed for conventional oil in vehicles that were common decades ago. Modern engines and modern oil formulations have extended typical service intervals significantly. But "extended interval" does not mean the same thing in Florida that it means in Minnesota.

Here's how to think about oil change frequency for Deltona drivers:

Conventional oil: If your vehicle takes conventional oil, sticking to an interval between 3,000 and 5,000 miles is prudent for Florida's summer conditions. The heat-driven oxidation that conventional oil experiences here can push it past its useful service life before a longer interval is complete.

Synthetic blend oil: A 5,000 to 7,500-mile interval is generally appropriate for most vehicles running synthetic blend in our climate, assuming normal driving conditions.

Full synthetic oil: Most manufacturers that specify full synthetic allow intervals between 7,500 and 10,000 miles under normal driving conditions. In Florida's summer, we typically recommend staying toward the lower end of that range — closer to 7,500 miles — rather than pushing to the maximum. The sustained heat exposure here justifies the more conservative approach.

Driving habits also matter. Short trips — under five miles — are surprisingly hard on oil because the engine never reaches full operating temperature, meaning water vapor and fuel particles that normally burn off in a warm engine stay suspended in the oil instead. If most of your driving is short errands around Deltona or commuting in heavy traffic, more frequent oil changes may be warranted even if your mileage is low.

When in doubt, ask your service advisor at LP & Sons. We'll look at your vehicle's service history, your manufacturer's recommendation, and your typical driving patterns to give you a specific interval that actually fits your situation.

Schedule Your Oil Change in Deltona at LP & Sons Auto Care

Your engine is the most expensive component in your vehicle. Protecting it with clean, properly matched oil — changed at the right interval for Florida's demanding summer climate — is the most cost-effective maintenance decision you can make. An oil change done right costs a fraction of what neglect eventually demands.

LP & Sons Auto Care is located in DeLand and proudly serves Deltona, FL and the surrounding Volusia County communities including Orange City, DeBary, and Lake Helen. We offer convenient scheduling, a comfortable customer waiting area, and the kind of thorough service that keeps our customers coming back. Every vehicle we service is backed by our commitment to quality — and every oil change comes with a full multipoint inspection at no additional charge.

If you're a Deltona driver who is due — or overdue — for an oil change, don't wait until the heat takes a toll on your engine. Contact LP & Sons Auto Care to schedule your service today. We Know Your Car — and we'll make sure it's ready for whatever Central Florida's summer has in store.

How to Keep Your Electrical System Running Smoothly This Spring

March 10, 2025
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

As the weather warms up and we transition into spring, it’s essential to ensure that your vehicle’s electrical system is in top condition. The alternator plays a crucial role in keeping your car running smoothly, especially as we start using air conditioning more frequently. Here are some tips to help you maintain your electrical system and avoid any potential issues this spring.

Check Your Battery

  • Inspect the battery for any signs of corrosion on the terminals. If you notice any buildup, clean it off with a mixture of baking soda and water.
  • Test the battery’s voltage using a multimeter. A healthy battery should read around 12.6 volts.
  • Ensure that the battery is securely fastened in place to prevent any vibrations that could damage the internal components.

Inspect the Alternator

  • Check the alternator belt for any signs of wear or cracking. Replace it if necessary to ensure that the alternator can operate efficiently.
  • Test the alternator’s output voltage to ensure that it is charging the battery properly. A reading between 13.8 and 14.2 volts indicates that the alternator is functioning correctly.

Look Out for Warning Signs

  • Pay attention to any warning signs that indicate potential electrical issues, such as dimming headlights, flickering dashboard lights, or difficulty starting the engine.
  • If you notice any of these symptoms, have your vehicle inspected by a professional mechanic to diagnose the problem before it escalates.

Keep It Cool

  • As temperatures rise during the spring months, your vehicle’s electrical system may be under additional strain, especially if you’re using the air conditioning more frequently.
  • Keep an eye on the temperature gauge to prevent overheating, which can place undue stress on the alternator and other electrical components.

Schedule Regular Maintenance

  • Don’t overlook regular maintenance tasks, such as oil changes and engine inspections, as they can impact the performance of your vehicle’s electrical system.
  • Follow the manufacturer’s recommended maintenance schedule to keep your car in optimal condition and prevent any potential electrical issues.

Consider Upgrading Your Battery

  • If your battery is several years old or showing signs of weakness, consider upgrading to a higher capacity or more reliable option.
  • A high-quality battery can provide your vehicle with the power it needs to operate all its electrical systems smoothly.

Final Thoughts

By following these tips and staying proactive about maintaining your vehicle’s electrical system, you can enjoy a smooth and trouble-free driving experience this spring. Remember that regular inspections and timely repairs can help prevent major issues down the road. If you’re unsure about the condition of your alternator or electrical system, don’t hesitate to consult a professional mechanic for assistance.

Filters and Fluids: How to Maintain Your Car’s Fluids and Filters for Winter Driving

February 10, 2025
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

As winter weather sets in, it’s important to ensure your vehicle is ready to handle the cold temperatures, snow, and icy roads. One of the best ways to prepare for winter driving is by checking and maintaining your car’s fluids and filters. These components play a critical role in keeping your car running smoothly and safely during the harsh winter months. Here’s a guide to maintaining your car’s fluids and filters for winter driving, ensuring that your vehicle remains in top shape.

1. Check Your Engine Oil

Engine oil is essential for lubricating the engine’s moving parts, preventing friction and wear. During cold weather, engine oil can thicken, which can make starting the car more difficult and lead to poor performance. To ensure your engine operates smoothly, it’s important to use the right type of oil and check its levels regularly.

  • What to do: Before winter, check your oil level using the dipstick. If the oil is low, top it off with the recommended oil for your vehicle. Consider switching to winter-grade oil if you live in an area with particularly harsh winters. Winter-grade oils flow more easily in low temperatures, providing better protection for your engine when starting in the cold.

2. Inspect Your Transmission Fluid

Transmission fluid is vital for smooth gear shifting and keeping your car’s transmission system properly lubricated. Cold temperatures can cause the transmission fluid to thicken, which may lead to hard shifting or slipping gears. Low or old transmission fluid can also cause unnecessary wear on the transmission.

  • What to do: Check the transmission fluid regularly, especially before the winter season. Make sure the fluid is at the proper level, and look for any signs of contamination. If the fluid looks dirty or smells burnt, it’s time for a flush and replacement. Maintaining clean, full transmission fluid helps ensure smooth performance even in cold weather.

3. Ensure Proper Coolant/Antifreeze Levels

Coolant (or antifreeze) prevents your engine from freezing during cold temperatures, which is essential for preventing engine damage. In winter, the coolant mixture should have an appropriate balance of antifreeze and water to protect the engine against freezing temperatures.

  • What to do: Check the coolant levels and ensure the mixture contains the correct ratio of antifreeze and water (usually 50/50). Low coolant levels can lead to overheating, even in cold weather, so topping it off is crucial. If your coolant is old or dirty, it’s a good idea to flush the system and replace it with fresh coolant to ensure your engine stays at the proper temperature.

4. Check Brake Fluid

Brakes are especially important during winter when driving conditions can be slippery or unpredictable. Brake fluid allows the brake system to operate smoothly and provides the hydraulic force needed to stop the vehicle. Cold temperatures can cause brake fluid to thicken, which may impact braking performance.

  • What to do: Check the brake fluid levels regularly and ensure they are within the recommended range. If the fluid is low or looks dirty, it should be replaced. If you notice any issues with your braking system, such as reduced stopping power or a soft brake pedal, it could indicate a problem with the brake fluid or other components of the system. Get it inspected and topped off to maintain braking efficiency.

5. Replace Your Air Filters

Your vehicle’s air filters (engine air filter and cabin air filter) play important roles in both engine performance and interior comfort. A clean engine air filter ensures that the engine gets enough clean air for proper combustion, while a clean cabin air filter keeps pollutants, dust, and allergens from entering your vehicle’s interior. Dirty filters can affect engine efficiency, fuel economy, and the quality of air inside your vehicle.

  • What to do: Inspect both the engine air filter and cabin air filter. If the engine air filter is clogged or dirty, replace it to ensure that the engine is getting enough air. Similarly, if the cabin air filter is dirty, replace it to maintain the air quality inside the car. It’s especially important to replace the cabin air filter before winter, as it will help improve defrosting and keep the air fresh when using the heater.

6. Check Your Power Steering Fluid

Power steering fluid is essential for making steering easier and preventing the steering system from becoming stiff or hard to control. In cold weather, power steering fluid can thicken, making it harder to steer. Low or dirty fluid can also lead to increased wear on the power steering components, which can cause costly repairs if left unchecked.

  • What to do: Check the power steering fluid and top it off if necessary. If the fluid appears dirty or has a burnt smell, it’s a good idea to flush and replace it. Proper fluid levels will ensure smooth steering, especially when navigating snowy or icy roads during the winter.

7. Inspect Your Windshield Wiper Fluid

Windshield wiper fluid is vital for clearing your windshield, especially in winter when snow, ice, and road salt can obstruct your view. Regularly topping off your windshield wiper fluid is necessary to ensure that your wipers can clear the windshield effectively. In winter, it’s important to use a fluid designed to prevent freezing and provide a better cleaning action for icy conditions.

  • What to do: Check the windshield wiper fluid reservoir and fill it with a winter-ready solution. Winter wiper fluid is designed to withstand freezing temperatures and will help clean your windshield more effectively. Ensure that the wiper blades are in good condition and replace them if they are streaking or damaged.

8. Replace Fuel Filter

The fuel filter keeps impurities and debris from entering the fuel injectors, which can affect engine performance and fuel efficiency. Over time, the fuel filter can become clogged, causing the engine to misfire or reduce fuel economy.

  • What to do: Replace the fuel filter at regular intervals, as recommended by your vehicle’s manufacturer. Replacing a clogged fuel filter ensures that the engine receives clean fuel and performs optimally. This is especially important during winter, as your vehicle will rely on smooth fuel delivery to handle colder temperatures.

9. Check Your Differential Fluid

The differential fluid lubricates the differential, allowing it to operate smoothly and ensuring that power is distributed evenly to the wheels. In cold weather, thickened differential fluid can cause the system to operate inefficiently, leading to increased wear and tear.

  • What to do: Check the differential fluid, especially if you notice any noise coming from the rear axle or if your vehicle struggles to maintain traction. Topping off the fluid or replacing it as needed can help keep the differential running smoothly, especially in snow and icy conditions.

10. Be Prepared for Winter with Proper Fluids and Filter Maintenance

Maintaining your car’s fluids and filters is crucial for optimal performance and safety, especially during the winter months. By checking and replacing fluids and filters as needed—such as engine oil, coolant, brake fluid, air filters, and power steering fluid—you’ll ensure that your vehicle is running efficiently and ready to face the challenges of winter driving. Taking these simple steps can help prevent breakdowns, improve fuel efficiency, and extend the life of your vehicle’s critical components. Prepare your car for winter with regular maintenance, and enjoy a safe, smooth driving experience throughout the season.

Why Timing Belt Inspections Are Critical Before the Cold Sets In

January 10, 2025
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Timing Belts: Why Timing Belt Inspections Are Critical Before the Cold Sets In

As the temperatures drop and winter approaches, it’s the perfect time to ensure your vehicle is ready for the cold weather ahead. One critical part that often gets overlooked during regular maintenance is the timing belt. Your car’s timing belt plays a vital role in synchronizing the engine’s camshaft and crankshaft, ensuring that the engine’s valves open and close at the right times. If the timing belt fails, it can lead to catastrophic engine damage, making it essential to have it inspected before the cold sets in. Here’s why timing belt inspections are critical before winter and what you should know about this crucial component.

1. What Does a Timing Belt Do?

The timing belt is responsible for keeping the camshaft and crankshaft synchronized in an engine, ensuring that the engine’s valves open and close at the proper time in relation to the pistons. It plays a crucial role in engine performance and efficiency. A faulty or worn timing belt can cause the engine to fail, leading to costly repairs and potential engine replacement.

  • What to know: The timing belt connects the camshaft and crankshaft, helping the engine’s pistons and valves operate in perfect harmony. Without a properly functioning timing belt, the engine could suffer serious damage.

2. Why Is Timing Belt Inspection Important Before Winter?

Cold weather puts additional strain on many of your car’s components, and the timing belt is no exception. When the temperature drops, rubber and plastic parts—like those found in the timing belt—become more brittle and prone to cracking. This can cause the timing belt to wear out faster, and if it breaks, the resulting damage to the engine can be severe and costly.

  • What to do: Get your timing belt inspected before the temperatures dip below freezing. Ensuring that the belt is in good condition before winter can help prevent a breakdown caused by a sudden failure during cold weather.

3. Signs That Your Timing Belt Needs Attention

A worn or damaged timing belt can cause a variety of symptoms. While some signs may be subtle, others can indicate that the belt is on the verge of failure and needs immediate attention.

  • What to do: If you notice any of the following signs, it’s important to schedule an inspection right away:
    • Rough engine performance: If the engine is misfiring or running roughly, it could indicate that the timing belt is worn or slipping.
    • Unusual noises: A squealing or slapping noise from the front of the engine could signal that the timing belt is loose or has broken teeth.
    • Oil leaks: The timing belt is often located in the engine’s front, near the oil seals. Leaks around this area could indicate damage to the timing belt or surrounding components.
    • Check engine light: The check engine light may turn on if the timing belt is causing problems with the engine’s operation.

4. Timing Belt Failure Can Cause Major Damage

A timing belt that snaps or becomes severely worn can result in significant engine damage. Depending on your engine type, a broken timing belt can cause the pistons and valves to collide, leading to bent valves, damaged pistons, and even a cracked engine block. Repairing this type of damage is often expensive and time-consuming, sometimes requiring a complete engine replacement.

  • What to do: Replace the timing belt before it shows signs of wear to prevent catastrophic engine damage. This proactive approach will save you from dealing with the high costs of major repairs.

5. Timing Belt Replacement Intervals

Most car manufacturers recommend replacing the timing belt every 60,000 to 100,000 miles, depending on the model and engine type. However, even if your vehicle has not yet reached the recommended mileage, it’s wise to have it inspected if it’s been several years since the last replacement.

  • What to do: Check your vehicle’s owner manual for the specific timing belt replacement interval for your car. If your timing belt is nearing the recommended replacement mileage or if it’s been a while since it was last replaced, it’s a good idea to have it inspected before winter arrives.

6. The Role of Temperature in Timing Belt Wear

Winter temperatures can cause the rubber and plastic materials of the timing belt to become brittle, which can make the belt more prone to cracking or breaking. In extreme cold, the materials used in the timing belt can lose flexibility, reducing its ability to handle the stresses of the engine. This is why the inspection and maintenance of the timing belt before the cold season are especially important.

  • What to do: Have your mechanic inspect your timing belt for any visible cracks, fraying, or wear signs. If the belt looks worn or the material has become stiff, it may need to be replaced before the cold weather worsens the damage.

7. Preventing Timing Belt Failure During Winter

Preventing timing belt failure during winter is all about regular maintenance and timely inspections. Proactive care can significantly reduce the risk of your timing belt breaking in harsh conditions. Additionally, replacing the timing belt at the recommended intervals ensures that it remains strong and effective, even when the temperature drops.

  • What to do: Be proactive about timing belt inspections and replacements. Have your mechanic check for any signs of wear, and if the belt is close to the end of its life, replace it before winter sets in. Regular checks, especially before the colder months, can help avoid sudden, unexpected failures.

8. What Happens During a Timing Belt Inspection?

When you bring your car in for a timing belt inspection, the mechanic will typically check the belt’s tension, condition, and alignment. They will look for signs of wear, such as cracks, tears, or glazing. The mechanic will also check for any issues with the belt’s tensioners and pulleys, which could contribute to premature wear or failure.

  • What to expect: The mechanic will visually inspect the timing belt and perform tests to assess the belt’s condition. If the belt shows signs of significant wear, they may recommend replacing it. In some cases, the timing belt may need to be replaced along with other components, such as the water pump or tensioners, to ensure everything functions properly.

9. How Much Does Timing Belt Replacement Cost?

Timing belt replacement can be a costly job due to the labor involved, especially for front-engine vehicles. The cost typically ranges between $500 and $1,000, depending on the make and model of your vehicle. However, replacing the timing belt before it fails is far less expensive than the repairs required if the belt breaks and damages the engine.

  • What to do: While timing belt replacement can be a significant investment, it’s a small price to pay compared to the cost of repairing a damaged engine. Timely replacement ensures your engine continues to run smoothly and can save you from far more expensive repairs down the road.

10. Be Proactive with Timing Belt Maintenance

Timing belts are essential to the proper functioning of your engine, and ensuring they are in good condition is crucial for maintaining the performance and longevity of your vehicle. By scheduling a timing belt inspection before the cold weather hits, you can prevent unexpected breakdowns, save money on costly repairs, and ensure that your engine runs smoothly throughout the winter. Don’t wait until the belt shows signs of wear—proactive maintenance now can keep your vehicle in top shape and protect you from winter driving challenges.

Winter Engine Repair: Tips to Keep Your Vehicle Running Smoothly

December 10, 2024
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

As the temperatures drop and snow begins to fall, it’s important to make sure your vehicle is in top condition to handle the winter weather. One crucial aspect of winter vehicle maintenance is ensuring that your engine is in optimal working condition. Cold weather can be tough on engines, but with proper care and maintenance, you can keep your vehicle running smoothly all winter long.

Signs of Engine Trouble in Cold Weather

Before we dive into tips for winter engine repair, let’s first discuss some common signs of engine trouble that you might encounter in cold weather:

  • Hard Starting: If your engine is struggling to turn over or takes longer to start in the cold, it could be a sign of a weak battery or other issues.
  • Rough Idling: An engine that idles roughly or stalls frequently in cold weather may have issues with the fuel system, spark plugs, or other components.
  • Lack of Power: A noticeable decrease in engine power when driving in cold weather could indicate problems with the fuel system, filters, or other engine components.
  • Strange Noises: Unusual noises coming from the engine, such as knocking or tapping sounds, could be a sign of low oil levels or other issues that need attention.

If you notice any of these signs, it’s important to have your engine inspected by a professional auto mechanic to diagnose and address the problem promptly.

Winter Engine Repair Tips

To help prevent engine trouble in the winter months and keep your vehicle running smoothly, here are some essential tips for winter engine repair:

1. Check and Change Your Oil

Cold weather can cause oil to thicken, making it harder for your engine to operate smoothly. Make sure to check your oil levels regularly and change the oil according to your vehicle manufacturer’s recommended schedule.

2. Test Your Battery

A weak battery can struggle to start your engine in cold weather. Have your battery tested to ensure it’s in good condition and able to provide the necessary power to start your vehicle reliably.

3. Inspect and Replace Spark Plugs

Worn or dirty spark plugs can lead to poor engine performance, especially in cold weather. Inspect your spark plugs regularly and replace them if needed to keep your engine running efficiently.

4. Check Your Coolant Levels

Antifreeze is essential for preventing your engine from freezing in cold temperatures. Make sure your coolant levels are adequate and that the mixture is correct to protect your engine from the cold.

5. Monitor Your Belts and Hoses

Cold weather can cause belts and hoses to become brittle and prone to cracking. Inspect these components for any signs of wear or damage and replace them as necessary to avoid potential engine problems.

6. Keep Your Fuel Tank Full

Running your vehicle on a near-empty tank in cold weather can lead to moisture build-up in the fuel lines and potentially cause engine issues. Always keep your fuel tank at least half full to prevent any fuel-related problems.

7. Schedule a Winter Maintenance Check-Up

Before the winter season is in full swing, consider scheduling a comprehensive winter maintenance check-up with your trusted auto repair shop. A professional inspection can help identify any potential issues with your engine or other components and ensure your vehicle is ready for the cold weather ahead.

By following these winter engine repair tips and staying proactive about your vehicle maintenance, you can keep your engine running smoothly and avoid potential problems during the colder months. Remember, proper care and attention to your engine can go a long way in ensuring your vehicle’s safety and performance all winter long.

The Importance of Alignment Services for Your Vehicle

November 10, 2024
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Proper wheel alignment is crucial for maintaining the performance, safety, and longevity of your vehicle. Misaligned wheels can lead to a variety of issues such as uneven tire wear, poor fuel efficiency, and compromised handling. In this article, we will discuss the importance of alignment services for your vehicle and why you should make it a priority.

Why Alignment Matters

  • Extend Tire Life: Proper alignment ensures that your tires wear evenly, extending their lifespan and saving you money in the long run.
  • Improved Fuel Efficiency: Misaligned wheels can cause increased rolling resistance, leading to decreased fuel efficiency. Aligning your wheels can help improve gas mileage.
  • Enhanced Safety: Proper alignment enhances your vehicle’s handling and stability, reducing the risk of accidents on the road.
  • Better Performance: A well-aligned vehicle drives smoother and responds better to steering inputs, providing a more comfortable driving experience.
  • Prevent Costly Repairs: Ignoring alignment issues can lead to more serious problems with your suspension and steering components, resulting in costly repairs down the line.

Signs Your Vehicle Needs Alignment

  • Uneven Tire Wear: Excessive wear on the inside or outside of your tires is a common sign of misalignment.
  • Vehicle Pulling: If your car tends to pull to one side while driving straight, it may be due to alignment issues.
  • Steering Wheel Off-Center: A crooked steering wheel when driving straight is a clear indicator of misalignment.
  • Vibrations While Driving: Misaligned wheels can cause vibrations and shaking in the steering wheel or seat.

The Alignment Process

Alignment services typically involve adjusting the angles of the wheels to meet the manufacturer’s specifications. There are three primary alignment angles to consider:

  • Camber: The angle of the wheel when viewed from the front of the vehicle. Positive or negative camber can cause uneven tire wear.
  • Toe: The angle of the wheels when viewed from above. Toe-in or toe-out misalignment can affect handling and tire wear.
  • Caster: The angle of the steering axis when viewed from the side. Proper caster alignment is crucial for steering stability.

When to Schedule Alignment Services

It is recommended to have your vehicle’s alignment checked at least once a year or if you notice any of the signs mentioned above. Additionally, alignment should be performed after certain events such as:

  • New Tire Installation: Whenever you install new tires, it’s essential to have your alignment checked to ensure even wear.
  • Suspension Repairs: Any repairs or modifications to your suspension system can affect alignment and should be followed by a wheel alignment.

Final Thoughts

Alignment services are an essential aspect of vehicle maintenance that should not be overlooked. Regular alignment checks can help you save money on tires, improve fuel efficiency, and ensure a safe and comfortable driving experience. If you notice any signs of misalignment or if it has been a while since your last alignment check, schedule an appointment with your trusted auto repair shop to have your wheels properly aligned. Your vehicle and wallet will thank you in the long run!

Signs Of A Failing Radiator: When To Seek Immediate Attention

May 10, 2024
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Your vehicle’s radiator keeps the engine cool and prevents overheating. However, like any automotive component, radiators can develop issues over time. Recognizing the signs of a failing radiator is essential for addressing problems promptly and avoiding costly repairs.

Engine Overheating

One of the most apparent signs of a failing radiator is engine overheating. If your temperature gauge consistently reads higher than usual or you notice steam under the hood, the radiator may not function correctly.

Coolant Leaks

Visible coolant leaks are a telltale sign of radiator problems. Puddles of coolant under your vehicle, a noticeable drop in coolant levels without any apparent leaks elsewhere, or a sweet smell of coolant inside the cabin all point to potential radiator leaks.

Discolored Coolant

Inspect the color and condition of your coolant regularly. Healthy coolant should be bright and translucent, typically green, orange, or red, depending on the type used in your vehicle. Discolored or contaminated coolant, such as a rusty brown color, may indicate internal corrosion in the radiator.

Rust or Corrosion

Visible signs of rust or corrosion on the radiator’s exterior indicate potential problems. Rust weakens the radiator’s structure and can lead to leaks or other issues if left unaddressed.

Coolant Contamination

Oil or other contaminants mixing with the coolant can signal a failing radiator. If you notice oil slicks or a milky residue in the coolant, it may indicate a leak from the transmission cooler or a cracked radiator tank, requiring immediate attention.

Sludge Buildup

Excessive sludge or debris in the coolant system can restrict flow and reduce cooling efficiency. If you observe sludge buildup in the radiator or coolant reservoir, it’s a sign that the system needs flushing and cleaning to prevent further damage.

Recognizing the signs of a failing radiator is crucial for maintaining your vehicle’s health and performance. If you notice any of these symptoms, don’t delay-seek professional inspection and repair to address the issue before it escalates into more significant problems.

What Is An Alternator?

An alternator is a small but mighty component in your car’s engine bay. Its primary function is to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy that powers various components in your vehicle such as lights, radio, and air conditioning. Without a functioning alternator, your battery would quickly drain itself out and leave you stranded on the road.

Signs Of A Failing Alternator

Dim or flickering headlights- If your headlights become dimmer or flickering while driving, it could be a sign that your alternator is not charging the battery correctly.

Strange noises- A faulty alternator may produce grinding or whining noises.

Warning light- Most modern cars have an indicator light on the dashboard that illuminates when there is an issue with the charging system.

Difficulty starting- If you are having trouble starting your car or if it stalls frequently, it could be due to a failing alternator.

Dead battery- Several factors could cause a dead battery, but if you have recently replaced it and are still experiencing issues, it could be due to a failing alternator.

The Importance Of Timely Repairs/Replacements

Ignoring signs of a failing alternator can lead to more severe problems. A malfunctioning alternator can cause other components in your car to fail and put unnecessary strain on your engine. Not only will this be costly to repair, but it can also leave you stranded on the road. Promptly address any issues with your alternator to avoid further damage and inconvenience.

If you suspect your alternator is failing, it is best to take your car to a professional mechanic for diagnosis and repair. Attempting to fix it yourself could cause more harm than good and cost you more in the long run. A skilled mechanic can diagnose the issue accurately and either repair or replace the alternator if necessary.

Purchasing a used car can feel like a gamble. You never know what hidden problems may be lurking beneath the shiny surface of the vehicle. A pre-purchase inspection (PPI) before buying a used car is essential. We’ll discuss a PPI, its importance, and what to expect during the inspection.

What Is A Pre-Purchase Inspection?

A PPI is an evaluation of a used car before purchase. This inspection is typically conducted by a certified mechanic who thoroughly examines the vehicle from top to bottom, checking for any issues. These inspections often include a test drive, a visual inspection of the car’s exterior and interior, and an evaluation of the engine, transmission, brakes, and suspension systems.

Why Is A Pre-Purchase Inspection Important?

Getting a PPI can save you a lot of money and trouble. Imagine buying a used car only to discover hidden issues a few months later that cost thousands of dollars to repair. A PPI can catch these problems before buying the vehicle, allowing you to negotiate a better price or walk away from a lemon altogether.

What To Expect During The Inspection

During a PPI, a mechanic will spend several hours evaluating the car. They will look for wear and tear, rust, leaks, and other issues that could indicate more significant problems. The mechanic may also use diagnostic tools to test the car’s engine, brakes, and other systems. Once the inspection is complete, the mechanic will provide a detailed report outlining any issues found and their severity.

How To Find A Mechanic For A Ppi

To find a good mechanic for a PPI, start by asking for recommendations from friends, family, and coworkers. You can also check online reviews and ratings for local mechanics. Look for a certified, experienced mechanic who specializes in the make and model of the car you’re interested in buying.

Buying a used car can be a great way to save money, but it’s essential to be smart about your purchase. A pre-purchase inspection can help you avoid unexpected and expensive repairs. By being proactive and getting a PPI before buying a used car, you can make an informed decision and have peace of mind when driving off the lot. Remember to find a certified and experienced mechanic for your PPI, and don’t be afraid to ask questions.

Keep Your Cool: Understanding Cooling System Repair

Your vehicle’s cooling system is essential in keeping your engine running smoothly. It prevents overheating, especially in warmer climates or driving for extended periods. But just like any other component of your car, it can break down and need maintenance or repair. We will provide you with the essential information to understand cooling system repair, so you know when to take your car to the shop.

Basic Components Of A Cooling System

The cooling system comprises several components: the radiator, water pump, thermostat, hoses, and coolant. The radiator core helps dissipate heat from the engine and pushes it away from the engine to operate in the desired temperature range. The water pump enables coolant circulation throughout the system, and the thermostat regulates coolant flow depending on the engine’s temperature. The hoses transport the coolant to and from the engine.

Symptoms Of Cooling System Problems

Your car may exhibit warning signs of cooling system damage, such as the temperature gauge rising above the normal range, steam or smoke from the engine, or a sweet smell of coolant. If you observe any of these symptoms, take your vehicle to a trusted auto repair shop as soon as possible.

Common Cooling System Repairs

Several issues can affect your car’s cooling system, including a clogged radiator, a faulty water pump, or a blown head gasket. Clogs in the radiator can prevent proper heat transfer, leading to overheating. An issue with the water pump can restrict coolant flow, causing heat buildup and engine damage. A damaged head gasket can lead to coolant leaks, which can cause severe engine damage. A professional auto mechanic can accurately diagnose the issue and recommend the appropriate repairs.

Preventative Maintenance Tips

Proper preventative maintenance can help keep your cooling system working correctly. One of the easiest things you can do is to regularly check and maintain your coolant level. You should also periodically inspect your hoses and belts for signs of wear and tear. Coolant flushes and radiator cleanings help to flush out any debris that can cause blockages in your cooling system.

Your car’s cooling system is integral in ensuring your vehicle runs smoothly and safely. Whether it’s a minor issue or a significant malfunction, taking care of your cooling system is vital to ensuring your engine doesn’t overheat and get damaged. Knowing the basics of your cooling system, the warning signs, and the importance of routine preventative maintenance can help ensure your car runs as efficiently as possible. If your vehicle exhibits any issues related to its cooling system, make sure you make an appointment at a trustworthy auto repair shop and get your car checked out.

Engine Repair – All You Need To Know About Timing Belt And Timing Chain

December 10, 2023
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Cars are an essential part of our lives. They help us commute from one place to another, ease our lives, and improve our daily activities. While we all depend on cars, it’s crucial to know how to maintain them properly to extend their lifespan and keep them in good condition. A car’s engine can be a complex and challenging system to repair, especially when it comes to timing belts and timing chains. We’ll discuss how timing belts and timing chains work, their preventive maintenance, and when they need to be repaired.

Timing Belt

It’s a rubber belt that connects the engine crankshaft to the camshaft, which transfers the power, making the engine run. The timing belt controls the valves’ opening and closing; in other words, it keeps the engine parts synchronized. Following the recommended replacement schedule for your specific car is crucial because timing belt failure can result in extremely costly engine damage. The replacement interval for a timing belt depends on the manufacturer’s recommendations, typically around 60,000-100,000 miles. Other symptoms that indicate it’s time to replace a timing belt might include engine misfire, loss of power or acceleration, and rough idling. By replacing the timing belt at the manufacturer’s recommended interval, your car will continue to run smoothly, and potential future problems will be avoided.

Timing Chain

A timing chain serves the same function as the timing belt, but it’s made from a more robust metal material. Maintain the timing chain to keep your engine in good condition. However, unlike timing belts, timing chains do not require routine maintenance. Typically, timing chains are built to last the vehicle’s life, so unless there are any specific problems, there’s no need to replace them. Also, unlike timing belts, replacing timing chains is more expensive since it involves a more intricate process of removing old chains and installing new ones.

Preventive Maintenance

Regular checkups of the timing belt or the timing chain are essential preventive maintenance measures. You should also look for any signs of tensioner failure, worn water pumps, cracks, or other damage to the pulleys. Rust and corrosion at the edges of the timing belts or chains can also indicate damage, so if detected, the belts and the chains need to be replaced.

When to Repair

Above everything, it’s vital to listen to the sounds that your car makes. Unusual rattling noises coming from the engine can indicate damage to the timing belt or chain. Other signs of a timing issue include inconsistent engine performance, high-pitched squealing, slipping gears, or difficulty starting your car. If you see any of these signs, take your vehicle to a professional mechanic to have it inspected.

When it comes to maintaining your car, the timing belt and timing chain are crucial components of the engine. It’s vital to keep them in good condition to prevent engine damage or repair and maintain your vehicle running smoothly. Always have your car regularly checked by a professional mechanic for any signs of damage or unusual sounds, and follow the recommended manufacturer’s intervals for replacements. By doing so, your car’s lifespan will be extended, and you’ll be able to enjoy your vehicle for many more years.

If you have red fluid all over the garage floor, you may have a power steering fluid or a coolant leak. This being said, we are going to talk about the transmission and this blog post because the transmission fluid is also red. If the leak is located underneath your automobile rather than the engine, the transmission is leaking. It is important to have this leak fixed right away to avoid damaging the transmission further.

Transmission Fluid Leak

A transmission fluid leak can come from a damaged transmission pan, a cracked pan gasket, a broken seal, or a cracked transmission hose. The red transmission fluid can also leak out of the torque converter. As we mentioned above, it is critical to have the leak fixed right away because the transmission fluid serves two purposes in the transmission.

First, the transmission fluid lubricates moving parts and removes dirt from them to keep them cool. Second, the transmission fluid is a hydraulic fluid so it can help the transmission shift the gears. If your transmission has a low level of fluid in it, it can become damaged as the moving parts wear against each other. The transmission will also struggle to shift the gears.

Resulting Malfunctions

Consequently, you may end up with an overheating transmission and grinding gears. This is a sign that the fluid level is too low and the moving parts are no longer protected. In addition, the gears are grinding because they are operating metal against metal without any protection. Unfortunately, this can wear down the gears prematurely and cause the need for a transmission overhaul.

In addition, you may start to notice other problems with the transmission. As we mentioned above, the transmission fluid helps a transmission shift through the gear cycle. If the fluid is low, you may end up with slipping gears or skipping gears. This can make driving your automobile nearly impossible, especially if the transmission becomes completely unresponsive.

The best way to avoid transmission fluid leaks is to keep up with the preventative maintenance for your automobile’s transmission. This means scheduling a service appointment with our shop every 30,000 miles so we can change the fluid and inspect the transmission. This helps your transmission last longer, and, more importantly, saves you money on expensive transmission repairs.

The Ultimate Guide to Catalytic Converters

July 1, 2023
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

As a car owner, you may have heard “Catalytic Converter” thrown around in auto repair shops. Do you know what it is, what it does, and why it’s important? It’s an emissions control device vital to your car’s exhaust system. We aim to provide a comprehensive guide to understanding what a catalytic converter is, its function, and how to care for it.

What is a Catalytic Converter?

A catalytic converter is a device that converts harmful gases from your car’s internal combustion engine into less toxic gases before they are released into the atmosphere. It’s a metal canister between your car’s engine and the muffler. The converter contains a ceramic honeycomb structure with multiple channels coated with catalysts such as palladium, rhodium, or platinum. These metals chemically react with exhaust gases, converting them into harmless gases such as water vapor, carbon dioxide, and nitrogen.

Why is a Catalytic Converter Important?

Catalytic converters are to reduce harmful vehicle emissions. Uncontrolled emissions from the car’s engine can adversely affect the environment and human health. A faulty or damaged catalytic converter emits a higher noxious gas mixture directly into the atmosphere, leading to air pollution.

Replacing Your Catalytic Converter

Replacing your catalytic converter can be quite expensive. The cost ranges from $500 to $2500, depending on the make and model of your vehicle. While you can replace the catalytic converter yourself, it’s advisable to leave it to the professionals as it’s quite complex, and failure to follow installation instructions correctly can lead to more damage or even injury.

Your car’s catalytic converter is crucial to your vehicle’s exhaust system. Proper care and attention can serve you for an extended period. So, it’s essential to take it seriously and adhere to your mechanic’s guidelines for maintenance. Remember, a little bit of care extends the lifespan and is suitable for the environment!

How Often Should You Change Your Oil?

The frequency of an oil change depends on your car type and the oil you’re using. Generally, most cars need an oil change every 3,000 to 5,000 miles (4,828 to 8,047 kilometers). What Happens if You Don’t Change Your Oil?

If you don’t change your oil regularly, it can lead to severe damage to your engine over time. Old or dirty oil can’t do its job effectively-it won’t lubricate the moving parts in your engine as well as fresh oil would-causing those parts to wear out faster than they usually would. Additionally, dirty oil contains debris and contaminants that can clog up the various components in your engine and lead to further damage or breakdowns. So although it may seem like a hassle now, regular oil changes are worth it in the long run!

What is Auto Diagnostics?

Auto diagnostics is an automotive term used to describe identifying and diagnosing issues with a car. This process uses specialized computer software and hardware to locate the cause of the issue. By plugging into your car’s onboard computer diagnostic system, qualified technicians can quickly pinpoint where problems may lie in the vehicle.

The Benefits of Using Auto Diagnostics

Using auto diagnostics has many benefits for both mechanics and customers alike. For example, auto diagnostics can save time and money by helping mechanics pinpoint the exact source of an issue instead of just guessing or replacing parts in hopes that it will fix the problem. As a result, customers won’t have to pay for services that didn’t need to be done or parts that weren’t necessary to solve their issue. Additionally, auto diagnostics can help mechanics identify potential issues before they become serious problems, saving you from costly repairs.

How Is It Used?

Auto diagnostics is typically used with other methods like visual inspections and mechanical tests to diagnose a problem with a vehicle accurately. A technician will use special software on their computer (IDATALINK Maestro or OBD2 Scanner Pro) that plugs into your car’s onboard computer system via a connector port under the dashboard. Once plugged in, technicians can access data that indicate potential issues with individual components or systems within your vehicle.

Auto diagnostics is a beneficial tool for mechanics and customers to troubleshoot automotive issues quickly and accurately. By plugging into your car’s onboard computer diagnostic system, qualified technicians can promptly diagnose precisely where problems may lie in the vehicle, so you don’t have to pay for unnecessary repairs or services to solve your issue. Regular maintenance, such as topping coolant levels and checking for wear and tear, can go a long way toward preventing unexpected breakdowns. Keeping your auto radiator in good shape is essential for maintaining an efficient cooling system and avoiding costly repairs. Regular inspections conducted by a qualified technician can identify potential issues before they become significant problems. With proper care and maintenance, you ensure your car’s cooling system remains in top condition.

What is an Alternator, and How Does it Work?

February 10, 2023
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

An alternator is a device that uses a spinning magnetic field to generate electricity. It’s usually used in automobiles but can also be found in aircraft, boats, and other vehicles. It provides power for the vehicle’s electrical system, such as the headlights, windshield wipers, radio, and other accessories. But first, let’s explore what an alternator does and how it works.

How Alternators Work

Alternators are powered by the rotation of their internal parts. The alternator contains two pulleys connected with a belt driven by the engine crankshaft. This causes the pulleys to rotate at different speeds; one spins faster than the other. As they turn, they create a rotating magnetic field within an iron core known as armature winding or stator winding. The rotating magnetic field creates alternating current (AC) voltage within two sets of windings on either side of the stator winding, referred to as the rotor winding or field winding.

Rectification is a process where AC voltage is converted into DC voltage through diodes or transistors by either an internal or external regulator, providing consistent output to electrical power components in your car. In addition, the regulator prevents damage to sensitive electrical components in the vehicle by controlling voltage production.

Alternators had come a long way since their invention in 1874 when Zenobe Gramme invented his first generator machine. Today’s modern alternators are more efficient and reliable due to their ability to self-regulate amperage output, depending on load requirements within a vehicle’s electrical system, and improved energy efficiency ratings.

Conclusion

An alternator is integral to any vehicle’s electrical system since it provides power for all its components while ensuring that too much current doesn’t flow through them and cause damage. Alternators have come a long way since their invention in 1874; today’s alternators are more efficient and reliable than ever before, thanks to advancements in technology over time. If you’re looking for an effective way to keep your car running smoothly and efficiently, look no further than investing in a quality alternator for your vehicle!

Symptoms of Exhaust System Problems

January 20, 2023
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Let’s talk about the symptoms of exhaust system problems so you know what to look for in your automobile. If your car, SUV, or truck is having any of the following problems, head straight to our auto repair shop so we can inspect the exhaust system to diagnose the issue. Once we know what is wrong, we will give you a detailed estimate to repair it and perform the work once you approve it.

Roaring Engine

If your engine has started roaring loudly suddenly, especially when you accelerate, you may have become the victim of catalytic converter theft. This not only removes the catalytic converter but also damages the exhaust system. A damaged muffler can also cause a loud and roaring engine.

Tapping Engine

If your engine is tapping, you probably have a leaking exhaust manifold gasket. This being said, the manifold itself can also leak if it cracks. This exhaust system problem is a rare one, but it can happen if you drive an automobile that has a ton of mileage and years under its belt.

Lagging Engine

A lagging engine is a sign that your exhaust system is clogged. Consequently, the exhaust cannot make its way through the system and out the tailpipe. Rather, it backs up into the combustion chamber and affects the combustion production. This will cause your engine to lag and perform poorly.

Lagging Economy

The lagging engine will also reduce your fuel economy because it will consume more fuel. This may seem strange because the engine is performing so poorly. Unfortunately, even if the engine is struggling to run, it will use more fuel than when it is running efficiently.

Damaged Tailpipe

Damage to the tailpipe is an easy occurrence because of where the tailpipe is located. The tailpipe puts up with a lot of abuse, especially if you drive your automobile aggressively. The tailpipe can be damaged by road debris, when you hit curbs, or if you get into an automobile accident.

Exhaust Smells

Finally, it’s a good rule of thumb to avoid driving your automobile if you can smell the exhaust. It is much safer to have your vehicle towed to our shop because inhaling the exhaust is poisonous. You may be able to smell exhaust inside your passenger cabin if the exhaust system is leaking.

Give us a call today so we can schedule an appointment that works well with your day to inspect your exhaust system. If there are any problems, we will fix them.

Problems Caused by an Old Water Pump

December 10, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

If you are having any of the problems listed below, it’s probable that it’s time to replace the water pump and or SUV, truck, or car. Do not worry. We can do this at our shop and get you back on the road as quickly as possible. A water pump is part of your automobile’s cooling system. It is the part that circulates the coolant through the engine. It tends to go bad after about 100,000 miles.

Leaking Coolant

One of the first things that happens with an old water pump is it will start to leak coolant. Usually, the leak starts off slowly, and you will not be aware that the water pump is leaking. Rather than drip onto the garage floor, the coolant will pool around the water pump and start to corrode it. If you see that your water pump is corroded, bring your vehicle to our shop so we can replace it.

The corrosion will eat through the metal housing of the water pump and create a larger coolant leak. In this case, you will see coolant on your garage floor at the front of your engine. This is where the water pump is located. It is important to have the pump replaced as soon as possible to avoid your engine from overheating. There may not be enough coolant in the engine if the leak is a bad one.

Poor Circulation

Age also reduces the water pump’s ability to circulate coolant through the engine. Unfortunately, if the circulation is reduced or nonexistent, your engine will overheat. The water pump can break down completely and, as such, the circulation of coolant will break down. When this happens, you will see the temperature gauge needle on the dashboard rise into the red zone very quickly.

Growling/Grinding

We mentioned above that the water pump is located in the front of your engine. If you hear grinding or growling coming from this location, the water pump may be making the noise. These noises are indications that the water pump’s internal parts are grinding against each other.

Overheating

Finally, as we briefly touched upon above, an old water pump that is not working properly will make your engine overheat. The engine is going to overheat whenever you fire it up until you replace the water pump with a new one to restore the coolant circulation and level.

We would be happy to inspect your water pump and replace it if necessary. Call us today for an appointment.

Signs My Engine Needs to Be Rebuilt

November 20, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Thankfully, there are options for those who never want to get rid of their cars. Engines can be rebuilt or replaced depending on the problem. We are a full-service auto shop with experienced technicians that can diagnose engine problems and recommend the proper course of action. You can count on us to repair your engine right the first time. Here are signs that your engine needs to be rebuilt or replaced.

Engine Exhaust

Rebuilding the engine often solves the problem of excess exhaust. If your tailpipe looks like a factory smokestack, the engine is hemorrhaging fluid. As such, the system in which the fluid is located may need to be rebuilt. If you are curious as to which fluid is leaking from your engine, take a look at the exhaust. Fuel creates black exhaust, coolant creates white exhaust, and oil creates blue exhaust.

Engine Knock

We can also repair a serious engine knock by rebuilding the cylinder block. Generally, the rod bearings need to be replaced because they have worn out. The part that is making the knocking sound is the pistons. If the rod bearings are worn, the rods will wiggle as they move the pistons up and down. Consequently, the pistons will make contact with the cylinder walls.

Metal in the Oil

Unfortunately, we cannot rebuild your engine if you have shavings of metal in the motor oil. In this case, we will have to replace the engine to solve the problem. The reason why is that the engine parts are disintegrating. Consequently, your vehicle needs a new engine.

Overheating

We can rebuild the engine if it is overheating all the time. This being said, the repairs may be extensive if the overheating is being caused by a blown head gasket or cracked engine block. The problem may also be the cooling system, which we can replace with a new one.

Power Loss

Finally, we cannot rebuild your engine if it no longer produces power. Unfortunately, this means that your engine is ready for retirement. There will come a point in time when the engine simply cannot produce any juice. Consequently, it is useless underneath the hood.

As we mentioned above, we have experienced technicians that can rebuild or replace your engine. If you are having any of the problems discussed in this blog post, call us to set up a service visit. To avoid damaging your engine further, tow your vehicle to our shop.

The Importance of Routine Auto Maintenance

November 1, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Many people view their car as simply a means of transportation – a way to get from Point A to Point B. However, your car is actually a complex machine that requires routine maintenance in order to function properly. Just as you would not expect a computer to work without regular updates and tune-ups, your car needs the same level of care. In this blog post, we’ll be talking about two important aspects of auto maintenance: fluids and filters.

Fluids

Your car has a lot of different fluids that keep it running smoothly. These fluids need to be checked regularly and replaced when necessary. The most common fluids that need to be maintained are oil, coolant, and brake fluid.

Oil is the lifeblood of your car’s engine and it needs to be changed every 5,000 miles or so. Coolant helps keep your engine from overheating and should be flushed and replaced every 30,000 miles. Brake fluid ensures that your brakes are working properly and should be flushed every 50,000 miles.

Filters

Just like your furnace has an air filter, your car has several filters as well – oil filter, air filter, fuel filter, etc. These filters need to be replaced regularly in order to keep your car running efficiently. Depending on your make and model, most filters will need to be replaced every 15-30,000 miles.

Regular maintenance is crucial for keeping your car in good condition and ensuring that it runs properly for many years to come. By staying on top of things like fluid changes and filter replacements, you can avoid bigger (and more expensive) problems down the road. If you have any questions about auto maintenance or would like help getting your vehicle ready for its next service interval, our team would be happy to assist you. Schedule an appointment today!

Signs My Car Has Suspension Problems

October 10, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Suspension problems can be a huge pain because they make driving your car difficult. They also make the ride uncomfortable for your passengers. We will talk about all of this below. Suffice it to say, if you are having any of the problems listed below, call our shop to set up a service appointment. We can repair your suspension and restore your comfort.

Uncomfortable Ride

Specifically, it is the struts and shocks that make it comfortable to drive and ride in your automobile. These suspension parts are filled with fluid that is designed to help them absorb the bumps in the road. If your vehicle’s ride is extremely bouncy, it’s time to replace the struts and shocks.

Lunging/Leaning

If your vehicle lunges, leans, or tilts depending on your driving action, this, too, is a sign that the struts and shocks have worn out. Normally, the struts and shocks will prevent your vehicle from leaning backward when you accelerate. This is just one example of the momentum motion that the struts and shocks prevent when they are in excellent shape.

Leaking Struts/Shocks

If you suspect that your struts and/or shocks are going bad, you can always check them to see if they are leaking. Just reach up behind the wheel and touch the suspension part. If it feels greasy or oily, it’s probable that it is leaking its internal fluid due to a broken seal.

Steering Difficulties

Another sign that your car has suspension problems is difficulty steering the vehicle. This can be an indication that one of the axles is cracked. It is also an indication of a problem with the steering system. It can be dangerous to drive your vehicle if you are having problems steering it.

Veering to the Side

Another thing that makes it difficult to control your automobile is if it veers to the side constantly. This is an indication that you need to have the wheels realigned. Unfortunately, they have been knocked out of alignment or have become misaligned naturally.

Low-Sitting Corner

Finally, your automobile should sit level on all four tires. If it sits lower on one of the tires, the leaf spring on that wheel is broken. In addition to sitting low, the wheel will also clunk whenever you go over a bump. In fact, a loud clunk is a sign that the suspension spring broke.

As we mentioned above, we can service or repair your suspension. Call us today to schedule an appointment.

LP & Sons Auto Care Can Repair the Cause of the Following Engine Noises

September 20, 2022
Leads Near Me User

We get it here at LP & Sons Auto Care. Strange noises coming from the engine can be frightening. You don’t know what’s wrong, and you don’t know if it’s safe to continue to drive your automobile. Bring your car, truck, or UV to our shop so we can inspect the engine to determine what is causing the noise. Once we know what the problem is, we will give you a detailed estimate to repair it.

Grinding

It’s probably a good idea to have your automobile towed to our shop if the engine is grinding. This usually points to an oil delivery problem. You may not have enough oil in the engine, or the oil pressure may be too low. Either way, operating the vehicle can damage the engine beyond repair.

Knocking

Another thing that can indicate that the engine is being damaged is a knocking sound coming from it. This could be a sign that the rod bearings are worn in the cylinder block. Because they are worn, they will jiggle the rods that move the pistons up and down inside the cylinders. This can cause damage to the cylinder walls. Your engine may also knock if there is a fuel detonation problem.

Popping

Popping usually points to an issue with the exhaust system. For example, the catalytic converter may pop if it gets clogged by carbon and sulfur deposits. In addition, the exhaust may make a popping or tapping sound if it is leaking out of a cracked exhaust manifold or manifold gasket.

Roaring

If your engine is roaring or rumbling like a racecar, you probably have a hole in the muffler. Unfortunately, this loud sound can also be a sign that somebody took it upon themselves to help themselves to your catalytic converter from underneath the automobile.

Sizzling

If your engine is sizzling and reminding you of your favorite Saturday morning breakfast, it’s possible that fluid is leaking onto hot engine parts. You could damage your engine if you keep driving it depending on which fluid is leaking. For example, you don’t want to drive the automobile if there is an oil leak.

Squealing

Finally, the engine may squeal if the accessory belt needs to be tightened or replaced. It can also squeal if the belt’s tensioner needs to be replaced.

An AC Service Beats the Florida Heat

September 1, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

Bring your car, UV, or truck to LP & Sons Auto Care for an AC service to keep the interior as cold as possible during the hot and humid summer weather. This is one way you can beat the Florida heat. We are also going to list some suggestions below that can reduce the temperature some inside your vehicle. Please keep in mind that these suggestions will not – not – reduce the temperature enough to make the interior safe for pets and children, so do not leave the pets or kids in the car.

Park in Shady Spots

We realize this suggestion is obvious but any time you can park in a shady spot, you should. This helps keep the interior of the automobile cooler than if you are parked in the sun. Think about getting shade anyway you can. You may not be able to park underneath the tree, but there may be a public parking garage close by. If there is, take advantage of the shade that it provides, as well.

Use Artificial Shade

You can also create shade for the interior of your automobile. Use a windshield shade that is held in place by the visors every time you park your car. Purchase window shades that attach to the window with suction cups. These shades open and close for your convenience. Finally, find a way to shade the rear window, as well, if you do not have an automatic shade in your vehicle.

Cover the Interior

Even with the shade, you may also want to cover the interior of your car, truck, or UV so it doesn’t scald you when you enter the car. You can do this with towels or rags. Simply drape the towels or rags over things like the steering wheel, the seats, the gear shift, and the dashboard to keep these surfaces out of the sun’s light. This keeps them cooler to the touch when you get in the car.

Get a Service

Finally, as we mentioned above, an air-conditioning service can take care of any problems your car’s air conditioner might have during the summer months. This includes replacing old refrigerant or topping off a low level of refrigerant, replacing a damaged condenser, or replacing a malfunctioning compressor.

In addition, it’s also a good idea to have the cooling system serviced if this is not been done in 30,000 miles. You want to make sure there is plenty of coolant in the engine to prevent it from overheating.

Why Has My Motor Oil Turn White?

August 15, 2022
Leads Near Me User
No Comments

LP & Sons Auto Care advises that if your car’s oil has turned white and looks like milk, it is being diluted by engine coolant. We cannot stress enough how important it is that you avoid driving your automobile if the oil is in this condition. One of the only things that can cause this problem is a blown head gasket, and driving your vehicle is going to cause additional and expensive engine damage. Aside from milky-white motor oil, here are other signs that you have a crack in the head gasket and it needs to be replaced.

Leaking Coolant

The head gasket creates a seal to prevent the coolant and oil from mixing and to prevent the coolant from leaking out of the engine. When the head gasket cracks, the coolant and oil mix and the coolant leaks into the combustion chamber. Also called the cylinder block, the coolant will mix with the fuel and air that is in each cylinder and cause combustion production problems.

Overheating Engine

In addition to the problems with the coolant in the cylinders, your car, truck, or utility vehicle’s engine is also going to overheat when you drive it because the coolant is leaking out of it. You can pour coolant into the radiator to no avail. Until you replace the head gasket, the coolant is going to flow into the combustion chamber. Consequently, your engine’s temperature will max out every time you try to drive the vehicle. Don’t drive it. Have it towed to our shop.

Bubbling Coolant

In fact, it’s best to have the vehicle towed because the engine can get so hot that you will end up with bubbling coolant in the radiator. Once the coolant reaches boiling temperatures, it will blow the radiator cap off of the radiator and spray all over the engine. If you have ever seen this happen, you know that the coolant sprays all over the place and can easily burn someone who is standing in front of the engine. If your coolant is boiling, turn off the engine and stay away from it until it cools.

White Engine Exhaust

One final sign that you have a blown head gasket is white engine exhaust. As we discussed above, the coolant will make its way into the cylinders where it will be burned with the fuel and air. When this happens, it creates white smoke that flows out of the tailpipe.

LP & Sons Auto Care in DeLand, FL, would be happy to help, so ring us today to set up a visit for your automobile so we can inspect and replace the head gasket.
